result:
Perhaps it starts with the sound. The low, resonant hum of the refrigerator on a still night, a sound so constant it becomes the canvas upon which the silence is painted. You only hear it when you listen for it, and then you can’t *stop* hearing it. The world is full of these baseline frequencies—the distant thrum of traffic, the sigh of central heating, the infinitesimal crackle of your own synapses. We are creatures navigating a sea of vibrations, mistaking our tiny, subjective raft for the ocean itself.

This brings me to windows. Not the digital ones, but the old, imperfect panes of glass. On a cold morning, they bloom with fractal gardens of frost, each crystal a unique conspiracy of temperature and humidity. You can trace them with a fingernail, melting temporary rivers through those frozen forests. A window is a boundary that pretends not to be one. It offers the illusion of connection—you can see the rain, but you cannot feel it. You witness the storm, but you are separate, a curator of the experience. Is that not the fundamental condition of consciousness? To be behind the glass, watching the weather of existence pass by, our feelings merely the breath we fog the pane with.

There’s a certain weight to afternoons in empty houses. Time, which in company flows like a river, here congeals into something viscous, like honey. You move through it slowly, and it leaves a golden, lingering residue on everything you do. In such solitude, you become aware of your own ghost—the imprint of where you’ve just been sitting, the echo of your steps preceding you. You are both the occupant and the observer. You might catch your reflection in the dark screen of a monitor and, for a fractured second, not recognize the arrangement of features as your own. Who is that person? What is it like to be them, right now, in this quiet room?

Which leads, inevitably, to hands. Look at yours. These are the primary instruments of your interface with reality. They have lifted, broken, caressed, created. They bear the maps of old scars—the faint white line from a childhood slip with a paring knife, the callus from a pen held too tightly during exams decades ago. They are aging faster than the rest of you, it seems. They remember the weight of a lover’s hand, the texture of a dog’s fur, the cool smoothness of a worry stone. They are archives of touch. Yet, right now, they type on sterile keys, translating the nebulous weather of thought into tidy, digital code. Another form of window.

We are all compiling our own private mythologies. The street corner that always smells of baking bread and therefore feels like a promise. The specific song that was playing when your heart first broke, now forever tinted with that ache. The inexplicable comfort of a particular shade of blue on a front door. We stitch these fragments into a quilt we wrap around ourselves for meaning. We call it memory, identity, soul. But isn’t it just a collection of sense-data, emotionally charged and narrativized? A story we tell ourselves to avoid the vertigo of the infinite, indifferent cosmos?

Yet, that indifference is also a kind of grace. The universe does not demand. It simply *is*. The stark, brutal beauty of a mountain range is not for you. It preceded you by epochs and will remain, unmoved, long after your name is utterly forgotten. There is a profound relief in that. It dissolves the exhausting pressure of centrality. You are free to be insignificant, and in that insignificance, find a pure, unobserved liberty. Your joys and sorrows are yours alone, not part of any grand score. They are therefore more precious, not less.

Consider the humble potato. Buried in darkness, blind and groping, it gathers nutrients, sends out shoots, becomes. We unearth it, scrub off the dirt, and see its gnarls and eyes. We subject it to fire or oil, transform its starchy flesh into something that sustains us. From dark earth to golden fry. What a journey. Every ordinary thing is the product of an epic. The cotton of your shirt was once a plant nodding under a sun you’ve never seen. The water in your glass fell as rain on a distant watershed. The iron in your blood was forged in the heart of a dying star. You are not just in the universe; you are a temporary, walking assemblage of it. A universe tasting itself.

Sometimes, the most profound communication happens in the gaps. The shared glance between strangers when something absurd occurs in public. The comfortable silence between old friends. The unspoken understanding in a touch on the shoulder. Language is a brilliant but clumsy tool, a net we cast to capture the sleek fish of meaning, which so often slips through the holes. So much of what matters transpires in the grammarless space between words.

And then there is light. The way evening sun slants through a dusty room, illuminating motes dancing in a beam, turning them into a galaxy of drifters. Each mote, a world. Each beam, a timeline. For a few minutes, the ordinary is sanctified, gilded. You know it will pass. The angle will change, the gold will fade to grey, and the galaxies will vanish. But for that moment, you saw it. You witnessed the alchemy. That’s the contract: nothing is permanent, but some things are breathtaking. You don’t get one without the other.

Regret and hope are perhaps the same emotion, viewed from opposite ends of the telescope. Regret is a longing for a past that could have been different. Hope is a longing for a future that could be different. Both are fantasies that pull us out of the present. Both are necessary, and both are thieves. The trick is to let them visit, but not to let them move in.

On the bus, you see them. The teenager, face glowing with the artificial sunrise of their phone. The elderly man, staring out with milky eyes at a world that is receding from him. The tired worker, head nodding against the window. Each in their own pocket universe, carrying unseen galaxies of worry, memory, and desire. You are just another one of them to everyone else. A background character in their story. This is the great democratic truth: everyone is the main character of their own epic, and an extra in everyone else’s. It should make us kinder. It often doesn’t.

Finally, the thought returns to sound. Not the refrigerator’s hum, but the sound of your own inner voice reading this. That silent speech in your head. Where is it? It has no location, yet it is the most intimate thing you know. It narrates, complains, wonders, remembers. It is *you*, and yet you can observe it, too. You can notice its tone, its rhythms. You can, sometimes, even quiet it. What is left when it stops? Not nothing. A vast, open awareness. A listening. The window, cleared of fog.

The mystery isn’t in the answers. The mystery is that there are questions at all. That this collection of stardust and water, arranged just so, can look at a frost-feathered window, feel the weight of an afternoon, recall the texture of a lost touch, and wonder about its own wondering. It can type words on keys, sending vibrations of thought into the ether, hoping they will find a resonant frequency in another collection of stardust and water, behind another pane of glass.

And sometimes, they do.
